text.tspan: DECOMPOSE BY BUSINESS CAPABILITY
(what the business DOES)
DECOMPOSE BY SUBDOMAIN (DDD)
(what concepts the business KNOWS)
PRACTICAL TRUTH: they usually CONVERGE
u2022 BC and SD often agree on the same boundaries
u2022 When they disagree, choose based on:
- Team structure (who's going to own it?)
- Rate of change (areas that change together stay together)
- Data ownership (clear data boundaries)
u2022 Bigger insight: AVOID DECOMPOSING BY TECHNICAL LAYER
u2022 BAD: 'database team', 'web team', 'business-logic team'
u2022 GOOD: vertical slices, each team owns end-to-end
Identify the major BUSINESS CAPABILITIES the org provides:
u2022 'Order Management'
u2022 'Inventory Management'
u2022 'Customer Management'
u2022 'Payment Processing'
u2022 'Shipping & Fulfillment'
Map each to ONE service (or small group of related services).
SOURCE: business architecture / org chart.
QUESTION: 'What does our company do?'
TYPICAL BOUNDARY: matches existing departments / teams.
Identify SUBDOMAINS of the problem space (DDD):
u2022 CORE subdomains (competitive advantage)
u2192 e.g. for Amazon: recommendations, pricing
u2022 SUPPORTING subdomains (need it, not differentiating)
u2192 e.g. customer accounts, basic catalog
u2022 GENERIC subdomains (commodity)
u2192 e.g. auth, email sending, payments-via-Stripe
Each subdomain becomes a BOUNDED CONTEXT u2192 one service.
SOURCE: domain modeling sessions with experts.
QUESTION: 'What are the distinct concepts and rules?'
TYPICAL BOUNDARY: where the same word means different things.
